audioscript.text: Section 1, Paul is renting an apartment.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: He has some problems with the property, and has decided to report them to the housing agency.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 and 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, MIC House Agency.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Good morning, I'm ringing about the problems I've been having with my apartment.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: If I can just take a few details first.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: What's your name?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Uh. Paul Smiley.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: How do you spell that?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: SMILEY.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Paul's surname is Smiley, so Smiley has been written in the box.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the conversation, and answer questions 1 and 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, MIC House Agency.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Good morning, I'm ringing about the problems I've been having with my apartment.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: If I can just take a few details first.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: What's your name?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Uh. Paul Smiley.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: How do you spell that?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: SMILEY.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: OK, and what's the address?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Uh. Apartment 2.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: 16 Rose Lane.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Rose Lane... and that's in?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: In Newton.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Oh yes, I know the property.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Uh. Could I just ask how long is the lease?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: It's for one year.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Huh?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: And you moved in?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Last week on 27th of June.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Fine, thank you.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Before the conversation continues, you have some time to look at questions 3 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 3 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And what are the problems that you've been having?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Well, no one thing is really dangerous or anything, but you know it's just been building up.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Well, the first thing is the washing machine.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: It's been leaking a little, and it's beginning to get worse.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Because we have a small child, we really need to get that done straight away.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: OK, that's a washing machine for immediate repair.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: And then there's a niggling problem with the cooker.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Ah ha...
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: The door's broken.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Uh. It's nothing serious and it can be used, but if you can send someone over in the next couple of weeks or so, that'd be great.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Fine, I've got that.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Then we are worried about all the windows.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Are they broken?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: No, but there are no locks on them, and you know with the insurance these days.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: And when would you like those done?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Oh, that's not really urgent,
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: but you never know when there's gonna be a break-in.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: No, we'll get those done for you next week.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Don't worry.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: And then there's the bathroom light.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: It's getting quite annoying.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: It flickers quite badly, and it's giving me headaches.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: I'd really like to get that replaced right away.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: That's no problem.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: And then the last thing on the list is the kitchen curtains, they're torn.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: We do have quite a few spare ones in stock, and can get those to you in the next week, if that's all right with you.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Yes, that'd be fine.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Anything else?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: No, that's all.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: OK, fine.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: What we'll do is get someone over to you this afternoon, if you're in.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Well, I'm gonna be out for a short time.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Well, you tell us your preferred times.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Well, the best time is about 1 o'clock.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: I'll have to check that with him, and if he can't make it then, what would be your second preference?
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Any time up to 5 pm would be fine.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: OK, I've made a note of that.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Great, well, thanks very much for your help.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: That's fine.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Thank you for calling.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: Goodbye.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Goodbye.
audioscript.speaker: AGENT

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

instruction: For the "When to be done" column, write A, B or C:
A. if the repair will be done immediately.
B. if the repair will be done during the following week.
C. if the repair will be done in two or more weeks.

items: workman to call between <input[]> pm and <input[]> pm

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a recreation officer at a holiday resort telling the guests about their accommodation and the activities available.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the first part of the talk, and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Great, well, hi everyone, my name's Jody, and I'm one of the four recreation officers here at Rainforest Lodge.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: My job is to make sure that you all have a great stay here with us and go away feeling relaxed and refreshed.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: As you can see, we're literally in the middle of nowhere at the lodge.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: There are no newspapers or TVs, and there's only one phone, and that's in the office.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: The Lodge is a complete 'getaway from it all' experience: a place to unwind and appreciate the world without a lot of interruptions and distractions.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: From your cabin balcony, you'll find that you can't see anyone else, and the only noise you should hear is the birds.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: When the luggage comes, one of the guys will take it across to your cabin for you, and make sure you know the way back here to the main center for dinner in the restaurant.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: Dinner will be served in about an hour or so.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: All the times of each day's activities are printed on the blue sheet you should have got in the information guides that were handed out on the coach.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: Each Explorer trip has a different focus.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: So it doesn't matter how many you do or on what day, because there's always something new to discover in the rainforest.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: Tomorrow, I think we've still got places on the Orchid and Fungi Tour.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: This is on foot, and takes you to different parts of the rainforest.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: Or, if you'd prefer, there's the Four-Wheel-Drive tour to the waterfalls, or the fishing trip where I promise you we'll catch some lunch.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: And last but not least, the famous Crocodile Cruise that leaves at 11 am each day.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: Just in time for the crocodile's lunch!
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: Oh, plenty to choose from here at Rainforest Lodge, or just sit on your balcony, relax and unwind, and enjoy the views.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: In the evenings, there is the Spotlight Tour, one of my favorites.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: The Spotlight Tour leaves at sundown, and lets you catch a glimpse of some more of the rainforest's wildlife as it comes out at dusk to feed.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: That's a great trip, and if you can, I'd really try to make sure you do it during your stay.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: You now have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the rest of the talk, and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You've chosen to visit the rainforest in March, which is just at the end of the wet season, so you'll soon notice how well the waterfalls are running, and also how damp the ground is.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: Things can tend to get a bit slippery too, so if you didn't bring any walking boots, I'd advise you to hire some from the office.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: You'll also be much better off in long trousers rather than shorts, because they will give your legs more protection.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: And socks are a good idea too.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: There's no need to be nervous of the rainforest provided that you treat it with respect and common sense.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: Most of the animals and wildlife are gentle and harmless.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: There are some venomous snakes to beware of, but really they're much more frightened of you than you are of them.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: The other thing is that certain plants can cause irritation if you touch them with bare skin.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: Well, that's about all for the time being.
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: The guys are here to take you and your luggage to the cabins...
audioscript.speaker: RECREATION OFFICER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

The guests are told to
options: carry their luggage to the cabin.

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS OR A NUMBER

items.title: What THREE items of clothing does the speaker recommend for the rainforest?
Which TWO things in the rainforest does the speaker give a warning about?

answer_key.correct_answer: waterfall | waterfalls | water fall | water falls | water-fall | water-falls

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear a conversation between a counselor and a student named John.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully to the conversation and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, John.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: What can I do for you?
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Well, I heard about these counseling sessions from a friend doing a science course, and I was really interested.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: I think they should be compulsory really.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Well, to be quite honest, John, I think they would be useful for everybody, but well, everybody has their own way of going about things.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: I prefer people just to drop in when they can.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: I find that talking to students about the requirements of a course helps to clarify what needs to be done.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: I mean the biggest difference between college and school is that new college students really have to do a lot of work on their own, and it's sometimes useful to get advice on how to take control of your time and work effectively.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Yes, I mean, it seems like a very light workload until assignment time comes, and then I seem to be working all night sometimes.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: I'm not the only one.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: It's ridiculous.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: The resource center is very good, but it closes so early.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: It's in the library, and so you'd think you could use it more.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: It's a real problem for me.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Well, you're certainly not the only person in that position.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: As I'm sure you've found, it really comes down to using every available hour in a systematic way.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: If you do this with a plan, then you'll find that you still have time for yourself and your hobbies as well.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, I've heard from Thomas that you made him a sort of plan like this, and he's going away for the weekend with all his work handed in.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Whereas I haven't even started.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: I need to find out a few more things about you first.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: I'll give you this form to fill in about your lectures and things before you leave.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: You now have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the rest of the conversation and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, what are your main problems?
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Well, what most concerns me is I'm still not doing very well in my assignments.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Well, I know that you plan your writing carefully, but this can come to nothing if the assignment doesn't answer the question.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: That really is the key.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: You must read the question carefully, and give it a great deal of thought before you even start planning or writing your first draft.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: It's also vital to check your work for errors.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Everybody makes them, and they can influence the person marking the work.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: So, always take time at the end to check what you've written.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: As far as listening is concerned, I find it hard to keep up sometimes in lectures, especially two-hour ones.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: I sometimes just seem to go off into a dream.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: It's a good idea to find out from your lecturers, if they mind you recording the lectures.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: You only need one of those small cassette recorders.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: The quality is pretty good, and a second listening can really clarify things.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Something else you can do is check your notes with a friend after the lecture.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Yes, that's a good idea.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: It's hard to do all that all the time, though, uh, especially when there's so much reading to do.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: It's important though, not to confine yourself to reading on your subject.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: You should also read things of general interest that appeal to you, you know novels, newspapers that kind of thing.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Do you have a good dictionary?
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Not really.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: I've never bothered with one.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: It would probably be a good idea to get one.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Dictionaries aren't expensive.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: And they can help a lot.
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: Also, you can underline or highlight new words, and then you've got a...
audioscript.speaker: COUNSELLOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: These sessions with a counsellor are
The counsellor says that new students have to
options: spend more time on the college premises.
work harder than they did at school.

items.title: John complains that the resource centre
The counsellor suggests to John that
options: he needs to study all the time.
he should be able to fit in some leisure activities.

options: talk with some of his lecturers.

items.title: WRITING

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear a student talking about his research project in a university tutorial.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the talk and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, good afternoon.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: In today's session, John Upton will be sharing some of the findings of his research project from last term.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: John.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Well, first of all, uh, a little bit about the background to the project.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Our title as you can see is pretty straightforward.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Car safety, but these days there's a lot more to it than the usual injunctions about drinking and driving or speeding.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: I'd been interested and horrified by several newspaper reports on what people call road rage.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: For example, the famous incident of a man getting out of his car in a car park.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: And hitting the driver of a van who had overtaken him earlier.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: It seemed to me that there were almost as many serious problems when cars were parked... i.e. were stationary... as when they were traveling at 90 miles an hour.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: So, I decided to make this the focus of the project.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: For our research, we depended mainly on talking to individuals, asking them questions, rather than using written questionnaires.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: We stopped people at a selected garage on the motorway over a two-day period.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: And asked them questions about what they'd observed or experienced themselves.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Our respondents were both men and women, but the women were just slightly in the majority.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: We were pleased by the public's willingness to stop and chat to us.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: In the end, we talked to a total of 135 drivers over those two days.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: So, what were our findings?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Well, as you can see, 93% of respondents had had some kind of problem.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: A surprisingly large percentage 24% had had their car damaged in some way, but the main type of incident was being shouted at.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: 79% had experienced that, 15% had experienced violence on their own persons, they'd actually been hit by someone.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: The police tended only to be informed when there was physical violence involved.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: So, what strategies had people developed to ensure their own safety?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Let's have a look at the figures here.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Well, first of all, it was quite striking that there were often distinct answers from the men and women.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: It was mainly women for example, who said one shouldn't ever stop to find out how to get somewhere.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Whereas it was men who said you should try to avoid looking directly at other drivers.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Both men and wom-, oh, sorry, no, uh, it was women who said you had to tell someone when you were due to get to a particular destination.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Then, I had thought that it would be mainly men, but both sexes made the point that it's much safer to get keys out well in advance as you go towards your car.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Men were very aware that muggers or whatever might be concealed behind the car.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: They also made the point that you should leave plenty of room when you park your car.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: So you can make a quick getaway if you need to.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Finally, locking doors at all times.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Men didn't think it was quite as important as women, but both gave it a high safety rating.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: When we asked them what they thought the best improvements had been in the last five years in helping with road rage problems.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: John was first interested in the subject because of something

items: Which pie chart shows the proportion of men and women respondents? <input>

items.title: The most common type of road rage incident involved

list: it was both men and women.
items: Avoid eye contact with other drivers.                 <input>
Inform someone of your likely arrival time.           <input>
Ensure car keys are ready when you return to the car. <input>
Leave plenty of space when parking.                   <input>
body.listTitle: Which group gave the following advice?
